questions.q: Identify the three core machine learning algorithm types named in the syllabus dot-point, captured by the mnemonic in this episode.
options: Linear regression, polynomial regression, and decision trees
Linear regression, logistic regression, and K-nearest neighbour
Logistic regression, K-nearest neighbour, and neural networks
Linear regression, K-means clustering, and K-nearest neighbour
questions.explanation: The syllabus names exactly three: linear regression, logistic regression, and K-nearest neighbour, captured by the mnemonic L-L-K (the two L's of the regressions plus the K of K-nearest). Polynomial regression is a fourth that the building dot-point adds ('L-L-K plus polynomial'), but it is not one of the named core three (eliminates A).

questions.q: State the defining trait of linear regression.
options: It predicts a continuous number by fitting the best straight line through the data
It predicts a category by outputting a probability and thresholding it
It predicts by finding the most similar known examples and averaging them
It fits a curve so it can capture relationships that bend
questions.explanation: Linear regression predicts a continuous number by fitting the best straight line through the data — output equals a slope times the input plus an intercept, with the slope and intercept being the learned parameters (weights). Thresholding a probability (B) is logistic regression, comparison to similar examples (C) is K-nearest neighbour, and fitting a curve (D) is polynomial regression.

questions.q: Explain why logistic regression is described in the episode as a 'trap that catches everyone'.
options: It is far slower to train than linear regression despite looking similar
Despite the word 'regression' in its name, it performs classification — it predicts a category (a yes-or-no), not a continuous number
It can only be used on data that already curves, unlike linear regression
It requires a service worker to run, which students often forget to add
questions.explanation: The name actively misleads: despite the word 'regression', logistic regression is classification — it predicts a category, typically a yes-or-no, by passing a weighted sum through the sigmoid to produce a probability between 0 and 1 and thresholding it. The marquee line is 'linear predicts a number; logistic predicts a yes-or-no'. Speed (A) and curving data (B) are not the issue, and the service worker (D) belongs to a different topic entirely.

questions.q: Outline the role of the sigmoid function in logistic regression.
options: It speeds up training by reducing the number of epochs required
It fits a curved polynomial line through points that a straight line would miss
It measures the distance between a new point and its nearest neighbours
It takes any number and maps it to a value between 0 and 1, read as a probability that is then thresholded into a class
questions.explanation: The sigmoid is the S-shaped squashing function that takes any number and maps it to a value between 0 and 1, read as a probability; if the probability is above one-half you predict yes, below you predict no. It is the same activation function met on a neuron last chapter. Fitting a curve (C) describes polynomial regression and measuring distance (D) describes K-nearest neighbour.

questions.q: Describe how K-nearest neighbour makes a prediction for a new data point.
options: It fits a straight line through the training data and reads off the output
It outputs a probability via the sigmoid and thresholds it into a class
It finds the K training examples most similar to the new point and takes their majority class or average value
It fits a high-degree curve that passes through every training point
questions.explanation: K-nearest neighbour predicts by finding the K training examples closest (most similar) to the new point and letting them vote (majority class) or average (numeric value) — 'tell me your neighbours and I'll tell you who you are'. K is simply how many neighbours you consult. Crucially it does not fit a line or a curve (eliminates A and D); it predicts purely by comparison.

questions.q: A weather app needs to predict tomorrow's temperature in degrees, and separately whether it will rain (yes or no). Identify the correct algorithm for each task.
options: Temperature is logistic regression; rain yes-or-no is linear regression
Both tasks use linear regression because both are about the weather
Temperature is linear regression; rain yes-or-no is logistic regression
Both tasks use logistic regression because both are predictions
questions.explanation: The tell is the output type every time: a number means linear, a yes-or-no (a category) means logistic. Temperature in degrees is a continuous number, so linear regression; rain or no rain is a binary category, so logistic regression. The trap is reasoning 'logistic, because it predicts the rain amount' — rain amount in millimetres would be linear, but rain-or-not is logistic.

questions.q: Distinguish between the fit and predict methods that every machine learning model object shares.
options: fit returns the prediction for a new input; predict adjusts the parameters from training data
fit splits the data into training and test sets; predict measures accuracy on the test set
fit takes the training data and learns the parameters; predict takes a new input and returns a prediction using those learned parameters
fit applies the sigmoid function; predict applies the straight-line equation
questions.explanation: Fit then predict is the universal shape of a model object: fit takes the training data and learns the parameters (slope and intercept, or weights, held as attributes), while predict takes a new input and applies those learned parameters to return a prediction. This is exactly the dot-fit/dot-predict interface scikit-learn uses. Option A reverses the two roles, which is the most tempting confusion.

questions.q: Explain what overfitting is and why polynomial regression is the perfect illustration of it.
options: Overfitting is a model too simple to capture the pattern; a low-degree polynomial forces a line through curved data

Overfitting is when a model fits the training data too closely, including its noise, so it generalises poorly; a high-degree polynomial can wiggle through every training point yet predict garbage on new data
Overfitting is using the wrong algorithm type; polynomial regression should never be used for prediction
Overfitting is when training takes too many epochs; polynomial regression always needs the most epochs
questions.explanation: Overfitting is when a model learns the training data too well — memorising its noise and quirks rather than the general pattern — so it scores brilliantly on training data but fails on new, unseen data. A high-degree polynomial can contort itself to pass through every single training point (zero training error) while matching only noise. Option A describes the opposite failure, underfitting — a model too simple to capture the real pattern.

questions.q: Explain why you must evaluate a model on a separate test set rather than on its training data.
options: Because the test set is always larger, giving a more accurate score
Because the training data is needed for the predict method, so it cannot also be scored
Because a model can memorise its training data, so a high training score tells you nothing; only good performance on held-out data it never saw is real evidence it learned the general pattern
Because the sigmoid function only works on data the model has not yet seen
questions.explanation: A model can memorise its training data, so scoring it on that same data tells you nothing about whether it actually learned the pattern — a pure overfitter can still get a perfect training score. The train-test split holds back a separate test set the model never sees during training; strong performance there is real evidence of generalisation, and a big train-much-greater-than-test gap reveals overfitting. The point is not test-set size (A) but unseen data.

questions.q: A team's regression model scores 98% on training data but only 60% on test data. Recommend the diagnosis and a fix, applying the episode's framework.
options: The model has underfitted; fix it by using a higher-degree polynomial to capture more detail
The training data was corrupted; fix it by retraining on the test data instead
The model has overfitted; fix it by simplifying the model — for example a lower-degree polynomial or limited tree depth — gathering more data, or applying regularisation
The model is working correctly; an 18-point gap between training and test scores is normal and expected
questions.explanation: The large gap between high training accuracy (98%) and low test accuracy (60%) is the signature of overfitting — the model memorised the training data rather than learning the general pattern. The fixes the exam wants are to simplify the model (a lower-degree polynomial, limiting tree depth/pruning), gather more training data, or apply regularisation. This is the same overconfidence that cost Zillow hundreds of millions when its overfitted model met a changed market, so the gap is not normal (D).
